responses: I lived at 6225 Mountainview Drive, Cheyenne, WY since 2005.  I am a 4th generation Wyomingite, born and raised in Lander. I have lived in Cheyenne for 28 years and in Fremont County for over 35 years.
68 (but whose counting?)

I am a graduate of Lander Valley High School and the University of Wyoming.  While growing up I worked on the family ranch feeding cows before class.  My first job out of law school was clerking for the district court in Lander.  In 1985 we moved to Cheyenne where I worked in the Attorney General's office.  Two of our three kids were born in Cheyenne.  We moved to Riverton in 1990 where I opened my own law practice.  In 2005 the family moved back to Cheyenne. I continue practicing law today. I have served in the Wyoming legislature since 2011 representing Cheyenne and Laramie County.  My service to the State has been one of the great honors of my life.

There are challenges and opportunities wherever you look.  To name a few:  Health care desserts coupled with ever increasing medical costs; community impacts from multiple property tax relief proposals with no backfill; Aging community infrastructure coupled with declining and aging populations and shrinking local revenues; fewer children and the less school resources due to legislative actions.  Despite such challenges, Wyoming remains one of the best kept secrets in America.  We have the people and natural resources to secure our way of life now and for years to come.  We have the lowest individual tax burden in the U.S.  Wyoming is consistently ranked as one of the best managed state governments in the country.  We do more with less.  We have a balanced and stable state budget, one of the best school programs in the world and developable natural resources to protect our standard of living and Wyoming way of life for generations to come.

We work on aspects of this problem every session. Health care related matters constitute the largest component of the State budget.  This is a all hands on deck issue. This includes ensuring adequate funding our healthcare workforce, reducing red tape to direct more time and dollars for direct care, expanding telehealth services and supporting rural emergency service districts.  We have funded new construction for the State Hospital in Evanston and Life Resource Center in Lander.  We have provide grants and matching funds of rural hospitals an clinics ARPA dollars for rural hospitals like the new hospital in Riverton.  Much more must be done, directly and indirectly.  Vibrant, attractive communities draw medical professional to Wyoming.  Maintaining existing services keeps providers here. All of us must continue to work together to protect our citizens and communities across the state.
No, with minor exceptions, such as adding state land to national parks (like the Kelly parcel) and potential small building sites to facilitate the construction of low-income employee housing.

We are the lowest taxing state in U.S. Our schools, local governments and special district rely on property tax revenue to provide essential services and maintain our standard of living for all of Wyoming. Further cuts will impact our ability to retain good health, quality education and essential services. Everyone loses.
To compete in regional and national markets, bring in new business, and help expand existing industries fostering economic development is a fundament building block for Wyoming's future.

Yes.  The political terrain in Wyoming has morphed in the past several years.  Out of state dark money involving negative, misleading, and often patently false statements are common place. Almost overnight it has become a multi-million dollar industry.  Transparency is essential to ensure fair and informed decision making by the public.

Cheyenne Wyoming, born and raised, I have lived here cumulatively for 26 years.

I bring experience in municipal government, cybersecurity, and the legal field. As a paralegal and having written legislation, I have conducted legal research, analyzed policy, and helped draft effective legislation. My background in government operations and technology gives me a practical understanding of today's challenges and the skills to serve our community with integrity and accountability.

Wyoming faces a shrinking and aging population, limited economic diversification, workforce shortages, and the outmigration of young people seeking opportunities elsewhere. In addition, we are grappling with access to healthcare, reliable infrastructure, and affordable housing.

I believe that addressing affordability is the key starting point. If we can ensure that people can both work and live in our communities, we can better retain young people, help fill workforce shortages, and strengthen access to essential services like healthcare. Improving access to healthcare, especially in rural areas, is critical to keeping families and communities healthy and stable. With a stronger population base, we can also expand our tax base, creating additional resources to invest in infrastructure, healthcare systems, and public services.

I believe the legislature can address this in several ways. One option is allowing the state to act as a reinsurer for health insurance providers operating in Wyoming, helping to stabilize risk and reduce costs. Similarly, the state could explore creating an opt-in public insurance pool, modeled after how municipalities pool resources to secure coverage.

Such approaches could increase bargaining power, improve market stability, and potentially provide greater flexibility in shaping rates and coverage options, while still preserving choice for consumers.

I do not support the sale or permanent transfer of public lands. However, I do support exploring alternatives such as land exchanges, long-term leasing, and cooperative management agreements that preserve public access while also allowing for responsible local economic development where appropriate.

I support addressing excessive property taxes through a combination of targeted relief and broader structural reform. This includes providing relief for homeowners, particularly those on fixed incomes, through tools such as homestead exemptions or circuit breaker programs that prevent tax burdens from rising faster than a household’s ability to pay.

At the same time, I believe we should pursue reforms that improve fairness and predictability in property tax assessments, helping prevent sudden or disproportionate increases in tax bills. We should also explore reasonable limits on excessive growth in property tax collections to ensure taxpayers are not overburdened by rapid increases in local levies.

I believe economic development works best as a partnership that combines the strengths of the public and private sectors bringing together the dependability and consistency of government with the speed, creativity, and innovation of the private sector.
The legislature should focus on creating a fair, stable, and predictable regulatory environment, while guiding investment in essential infrastructure such as roads and broadband and ensuring permitting and licensing processes are efficient and accessible.
The private sector is the primary driver of growth, leading through entrepreneurship, job creation, and innovation, and responding quickly to changing market needs.
When targeted incentives are used to attract investment or support key industries, they should be applied carefully, with clear accountability, transparency, and measurable results.

I believe campaign finance should be guided by transparency, accountability, and public trust. Contributions should never influence policy outcomes or create the perception that access to elected officials is for sale. Voters deserve to know who is funding campaigns and how that funding may be shaping political priorities.
I support reasonable and transparent campaign donation rules that allow for civic participation while ensuring full disclosure of contributions and expenditures. Public confidence in government depends on clear reporting, timely disclosure, and strict compliance to existing ethics laws.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
